artifacts.content:
Hi Rachel,

Finally sitting down after getting Sophie to bed (she is VERY invested in the tooth that is “definitely” going to fall out tomorrow). Figured I’d write instead of doomscrolling.

I just booked my annual skin check with Dr. Patel at the UW Medical Center dermatology clinic again for August, like we did after that whole mole thing in August 2019. I keep thinking about when she took off that mildly atypical mole on my upper back and then stood there in the exam room telling me that with my fair skin and our family history of “suspicious spots,” I have a slightly elevated lifetime risk of skin cancer and should be extra vigilant. It still echoes in my head every time I forget sunscreen on a cloudy Seattle day.

We’re talking about maybe a beach weekend next month, so consider this your nagging sister reminder: please bring real sunscreen for you and Oliver, not the half-empty mystery bottle from the bottom of the stroller. Dr. Patel’s voice is basically my conscience now.

On the anxiety front, things are…okay-ish. Do you remember that awful product launch at Pacific Horizon back in late 2021? Around then I had those two short-notice telehealth visits with Dr. Laura Chen at Pacific Primary Care because my generalized anxiety went through the roof. I even sent her that MyChart message on November 3, 2021 — subject line “Follow-up on increased anxiety and sleep issues” — where I described those panic-y nights and asked if we should bump up my sertraline. We ended up keeping me at 50 mg, but I still cringe thinking about how wired I was.

I had a mini flashback to that this month with all the restructuring talk. Elena is being cagey about who’s on what team, and my brain goes straight to worst-case scenarios. I’m trying to be proactive: walks with Luna, less caffeine, and maybe actually finishing that meditation course I started.

Anyway, enough medical musings. How’s Portland treating you this week? Send me a picture of Oliver in his dinosaur pajamas if you get a minute.

Love,
Emily

metadata.subject: Skin checks, sunscreen, and my overactive brain

artifacts.content:
Hey Michael,

Quick brain dump while I’m between Zoom sessions.

I’m still stewing about my 1:1 with Elena yesterday in Conference Room 5B. When we were talking about the upcoming client project with the San Francisco and Portland travel, she said — in that “I’m just being thoughtful” voice — that I might not be the best fit because “the stress and travel might not be great for your anxiety.” I can’t tell if I’m more angry or embarrassed. It felt like my diagnosis was sitting in the chair next to me, answering for me.

It’s extra frustrating because I’ve worked so hard to keep things stable since that spike around the big launch in late 2021. Remember when I had those two short-notice telehealth calls with Dr. Chen at Pacific Primary Care and sent that MyChart note on November 3, 2021 about the panic-y nights and not sleeping? We adjusted everything else in life so I could stay on the same sertraline dose, and it’s been fine. I hate that Elena seems to see “anxiety” before she sees the actual work I’ve done.

All of this is stirring up the baby question again, too. I keep looping back to that awful phone call with my mom in February 2022 from Tacoma when she told me she worries I’m “too anxious to handle another baby.” Then the email I sent her that night — subject line “About what you said earlier” — where I spelled out that I’m on meds and managing it with Dr. Chen. Between that and Elena’s comment, it’s hard not to feel like everyone has quietly decided what I can and can’t handle.

Also, I realized I never told you how much it bothered me that during my postpartum stay at UW Medical Center back in October 2018, they sent that social worker to Room 432 because my chart flagged anxiety. I remember sitting there, exhausted and crying, getting handed the “Postpartum Mood Support Resources – Seattle Area” packet and feeling like I was already failing at motherhood. Maybe that’s part of why my mom’s comment cut so deep.

Can we talk tonight after Sophie’s in bed? Maybe over tea, not wine — I’ve been noticing I reach for a glass on the really stressful nights more than I’d like.

Love you,
E

metadata.subject: That 1:1 with Elena and everything it stirred up

artifacts.content:
Title: March 2023 – pause on pill? (follow-up thoughts)

I just reread the email I sent myself last year with the subject line “March 2023 – pause on pill?” and I’m glad I captured the details.

Recap for future me: In March 2023, after that long conversation with Michael in the kitchen about maybe trying for a second baby, I stopped my oral contraceptive for one cycle. I remember standing by the sink in our Ravenna house, talking about how Sophie is getting older and whether we’d regret not at least trying. I restarted the pill in April 2023 after my anxiety flared and I spiraled about pregnancy risks at 36 — complications, sleep deprivation, going back to that vulnerable place I was in after Sophie’s birth.

Thinking about it now, I keep going back to that postpartum room at UW Medical Center in October 2018, Room 432, when the hospital social worker came in because my chart flagged a history of anxiety. I admitted I’d been crying constantly and feeling overwhelmed, and she handed me the “Postpartum Mood Support Resources – Seattle Area” sheet. I never followed up formally, but the memory still sits like a warning light in the back of my mind.

Layered on top of that is Mom’s comment in February 2022, when she called from Tacoma and said she was worried I’m “too anxious to handle another baby.” Then my late-night email back to her (“About what you said earlier”), where I defended myself and spelled out that I’m stable on sertraline with Dr. Chen.

All of this is tangled up with work, too. Elena’s recent comment in our September 2024 1:1 in Conference Room 5B — that I might not be “the best fit” for the SF/Portland travel project because “the stress and travel might not be great for your anxiety” — made me feel like my diagnosis is a label other people use to make decisions for me.

For now: staying on the pill, revisiting the baby question after things settle at work, and bringing all of this up with Dr. Chen at my next check-in.

Event: Annual physical with Dr. Chen – bring anxiety + allergy notes

Time: Tuesday, 2025-05-20, 12:30–1:30 p.m.
Location: Pacific Primary Care, Seattle – Internal Medicine (Dr. Laura Chen)
Attendees: Emily

Notes to self:

1. Anxiety check-in
- Remind Dr. Chen about the spike I had around the big product launch at Pacific Horizon in late 2021. I had those two short-notice telehealth visits with her then, and I sent that MyChart message on November 3, 2021 (“Follow-up on increased anxiety and sleep issues”) describing several nights of panic-like symptoms and not sleeping. We decided not to increase my sertraline dose at the time, but I want to confirm she still thinks 50 mg daily is right.
- Mention Elena’s comment from our September 2024 1:1 in Conference Room 5B, where she said I might not be “the best fit” for the travel-heavy SF/Portland project because “the stress and travel might not be great for your anxiety.” Ask Dr. Chen how to think about advocating for myself at work without minimizing my mental health.
- Bring up those March 2024 nights when I was so frazzled after back-to-back Zoom research sessions that I ended up drinking 2–3 glasses of wine alone in the living room while Michael put Sophie to bed. I even wrote that note on my phone on March 21 titled “need to watch the wine.” I don’t think it’s a huge problem yet, but I don’t want it to become my default coping strategy.

2. Allergies
- Ask whether anything needs to change after that May 2022 allergy flare when my eyes were so swollen and itchy I couldn’t use my computer, and I had to do an urgent same-day visit at Pacific Primary Care and miss half a day of work.

3. General
- Quick update on the March 2023 one-cycle break from my oral contraceptive and restarting in April 2023.

Subject: Remember those first weeks with Sophie?

Hi Jenna,

Your text from the postpartum floor made me want to teleport to Swedish and smuggle you a hot coffee and a nap. Since I can’t, here’s an email hug.

I keep thinking back to my own stay at UW Medical Center when Sophie was born in October 2018. I was in Room 432, totally wiped out after the epidural wore off, and a social worker came in because my chart flagged a history of anxiety. I remember sitting there in the hospital gown, crying while Sophie slept, and admitting I felt overwhelmed and was crying a lot. She was kind, handed me this packet called “Postpartum Mood Support Resources – Seattle Area,” and told me to reach out if things got worse. I never did, but that conversation is still very vivid.

It’s wild how those early days color everything afterward. Even now, when Michael and I have our on-again/off-again talks about a second baby, that memory is part of the calculus. In March 2023 we even went so far as to pause my pill for one cycle after a long kitchen conversation, then I panicked about pregnancy risks at 36 and restarted my oral contraceptive in April 2023. I literally emailed myself “March 2023 – pause on pill?” so I wouldn’t forget what we decided.

And it doesn’t help when family chimes in. In February 2022 my mom called from Tacoma and, in her “concerned” voice, said she worried I’m “too anxious to handle another baby.” I ended up writing her a long email that night (“About what you said earlier”) explaining that yes, I have generalized anxiety, but I’m managing it with Dr. Chen and sertraline, and that doesn’t make me a bad mom.

All of that to say: if you’re crying in your hospital room right now, you’re not broken. You’re a human who just did something enormous. Please be gentle with yourself and use the resources they offer if they feel helpful.

Text me any time, even at 2 a.m. I’m probably awake worrying about a usability study anyway.

Love,
Emily

metadata.subject: Remember those first weeks with Sophie?

artifacts.content:
Title: Coping habits – March 2024 reflection

I want to capture this honestly so I can look back with clear eyes later.

March 2024 was rough at work — back-to-back Zoom research sessions for Pacific Horizon, constant Slack pings, and the ongoing uncertainty about team restructuring. On a few of the worst evenings, I noticed a pattern: I’d log off, feel totally wired, and pour myself a glass of wine “to take the edge off.” Then another. On at least a couple of nights, including March 21, I had 2–3 glasses alone in the living room while Michael handled Sophie’s bedtime. That was the night I typed a quick note on my phone titled “March 21 – need to watch the wine” because I realized I was reaching for alcohol as my primary coping mechanism.

I keep reminding myself that I don’t use any controlled substances — no weed, nothing else — and overall I don’t think my drinking is out of control. But I also know how slippery that slope can be, especially with my generalized anxiety. It’s too easy to say, “It’s just a glass of wine,” every night.

This all feels connected to the way other people frame my anxiety. Elena’s comment in our September 2024 1:1 in Conference Room 5B — that I might not be “the best fit” for the SF/Portland travel project because “the stress and travel might not be great for your anxiety” — made me feel small. Same with Mom’s February 2022 phone call from Tacoma where she said she worries I’m “too anxious to handle another baby.” Those moments make me want to prove I’m fine, which sometimes looks like pushing too hard during the day and then numbing out at night.

Plan: bring this up with Dr. Chen at my next visit, be honest with Michael about the 2–3-glass nights, and experiment with other end-of-day rituals (walk with Luna, tea, stretching, actual downtime away from screens).

Hey HR Team,

I wanted to follow up on our conversation about sick time and flexible work arrangements, just to make sure I’m documenting things clearly.

Most of the time my health is pretty stable — I manage generalized anxiety with sertraline through my primary care doctor (Dr. Chen at Pacific Primary Care) and keep my seasonal allergies in check with over-the-counter meds. Every once in a while, though, things spike.

One example you’ll see in my records is May 2022. During a high-pollen week, my allergy symptoms got so bad that my eyes were swollen and insanely itchy. I literally couldn’t look at my computer screen without wanting to claw my face off. I ended up scheduling an urgent same-day visit at Pacific Primary Care in Seattle. The nurse practitioner there noted that I’d missed about half a day of work because of it. That’s the afternoon I emailed my manager with the subject line “Working from home this afternoon – allergy flare” to explain I’d been to the doctor and would be online later once the meds kicked in.

More recently, my anxiety flared around that big product launch in late 2021. I had two short-notice telehealth visits with Dr. Chen and sent her a MyChart message on November 3, 2021 titled “Follow-up on increased anxiety and sleep issues,” describing a few nights of panic-like symptoms. We didn’t end up changing my medication, but it was a reminder that crunch periods can hit me harder.

I’m not asking for anything formal right now, but I do want to be proactive and transparent so that if I need to work from home unexpectedly for a half day (like in May 2022) or adjust my schedule during a crunch, we’ve already had this conversation.

Happy to chat more if that’s helpful.

Best,
Emily Thompson
UX Researcher, Pacific Horizon Software

metadata.subject: Context on occasional health-related schedule changes

Health & Wellness Overview for Emily Rose Thompson

Patient Name: Emily Rose Thompson
Birth Date: 1987-04-19

Current Health Routines:
- Special Diet: Emily is currently following a Mediterranean-style diet, focusing on more vegetables and whole grains, which has positively impacted her overall well-being.
- Tobacco Use: Emily does not use tobacco, aligning with her focus on maintaining a healthy lifestyle.

Allergies:
- Emily has no allergies to aspirin, codeine, local anesthetics, acrylic, metals, latex, or sulfa drugs.

Medication & Supplements:
- Emily does not take or has never taken Phen Fen, Redux, or any bisphosphonates like Fosamax, Boniva, or Actonel.

Medical History:
- Serious Head or Neck Injury: Emily has never experienced a serious head or neck injury.
- Nursing Status: Emily is not currently nursing.

Notes:
- Emily's intentional health routines include daily walks with Luna, her golden retriever, and she practices meditation regularly to manage stress and her generalized anxiety disorder. She takes sertraline to keep her anxiety in check and consults with Dr. Laura Chen as needed.
- Seasonal allergies are managed with cetirizine and fluticasone nasal spray, part of her daily routine during allergy seasons.
- Emily continues to navigate the balance between her professional responsibilities at Pacific Horizon Software and her personal life, ensuring she maintains her health and wellness amidst a busy schedule.
